You're keeping the thing from turning into a bitch, moan, and complain session. A little bit of that's okay. I got it, but not a lot. You're watching the clock, so you're making sure... See, you can't have the last two people participating in your coaching group have eight minutes of peace, and everybody else had an hour. You can't have them like cheating, so somebody's leaving early all the time, or out making phone calls while somebody else is taking a turn, so that's no good. So I do stuff like, you know, if somebody's starting to abuse that, and now we start for a meeting until everybody gets their behavior back in shape, we draw for who goes in what order, and you don't get to volunteer and I don't pick you, or whoever left early last time, you're last this time. So you leave early again, you don't get to turn at all, and you're last. I mean, so you're playing cop, you know, you're making sure that everybody's having a good experience, that the game is well played by all, right? In a client relationship, your biggest building blocks of control are mostly ones we already talked about, the fact that they come to you rather than you go into them, that you control their access. Again, that you have rules. You've got to have rules for one-on-one clients, too. They've got to know what the rules are. How... So you can't expect them to behave well if you haven't told them how they're supposed to behave. And you can't call about it when they behave badly. So, like, I tell people to send me stuff by Federal Express. See, I mean Federal Express. I don't mean Pony Express, I don't mean DHL, I don't mean UPS, I don't mean Priority Mail, dammit, I mean Federal Express. Especially the handful of clients who have access to send stuff directly to me in Ohio when I am there and working on their stuff rather than through Vicky and Phoenix, because where I get my stuff in Ohio, only Federal Express manages to get it there. The DHL guy can't find the place. The UPS guy, some days he finds it, some days he doesn't, who knows? You know, Federal Express, the guy knows where I'm at, right? So I tell everybody, Federal Express, now I have the right, when some nitwit sends it the other way, to send them a fax and say, what part of Federal Express didn't you get? I said FedEx. Your problems now exist because you could follow instructions. But if you didn't give them the instruction to start with, you can't really be aggravated at them, and you're not exercising control. Now, by the way, if I had no practical reason for what I just said, the very act of specifying precisely the way or ways in which you can deliver things to me is an exercise of control, even if I had no practical need to do it. It would be smart to do it. Now, in this case, I have a practical reason to do it. I even have, like, sort of a necessity to do it. But even if I didn't, it would be smart to do it because I'm building blocks of control. And so if they have to go through a process, like, and they got to think to themselves, okay, I'm going to Dan's meeting. How am I supposed to behave at Dan's meeting? Which they never have that thought about any place else they're going. And, okay, I got to communicate with him. How am I supposed to communicate with him again? And they never have that thought about anybody else who has the most control over them. I got the most control over them. Because they're having to modify their behavior all the time to conform to my rules. Makes it easy to impose a new rule, by the way, too. If you've already had rules. It's real hard to impose a new rule if you haven't had any rules. Contracts. We're going to talk about this in depth later. And when I show you sample documents. But like, here's the big it about this. Paper. Don't do anything without paper. Verbal. It's amazing to me how many times I'm sitting with people who are in complex relationships. They're halfway into projects. They're partners in businesses. And now they got problems. And they got no contracts. Nothing.
